Date: 2025-02-11 09:42
Subject: Follow-up Info for Auto & Home Policy Review

Hi Laura,

Thanks again for taking the time to speak with me yesterday about reviewing our auto and home coverage while I’m on modified duties. I’ve pulled together the details you asked for so you can update your notes before we talk again on Friday.

For the auto policy, we’ve been with the same carrier for 3.0 years now, since we switched over in early 2022 when Emily and I refinanced the house. No at-fault accidents, and just that one small windshield chip claim last winter.

On the home side, it’s still just the one property at 27 Fairview Avenue here in Hamilton. My spouse Emily and I live there with our daughters, Sophie (5) and Lily (2). From January to March 2024, one of my coworkers from GreenLeaf, Carlos Ramirez, rented our basement after he and his partner Ana separated. He was technically part of our household during that stretch, but we didn’t really broadcast it at work because we didn’t want Daniel or Sarah thinking we were turning the house into a place for crew gossip.

Employment-wise, I’m still with GreenLeaf Urban Landscaping Inc., currently on medical/modified duties because of the lower back strain I mentioned. WSIB is covering part of my income, and my physio team is optimistic I can ramp back up by late spring.

Let me know if you also need Emily’s latest pay stub from St. Joseph’s or copies of our current declarations pages.

Best,

Michael Jiahao Chen
27 Fairview Avenue
Hamilton, ON  L8K 3N2

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Chen <m.chen27@gmail.com>
To: Group Benefits Team <benefits@greenleafurban.ca>
Date: 2025-02-03 09:42
Subject: Clarification on years with Sun Life for new enrollment form

Hi Sarah,

I’m filling out the updated benefits enrollment package you dropped off, and I’m stuck on the “number of years with current carrier” question.

We’ve had a few changes over the years, so I want to make sure I’m not mixing things up:

- I remember when Daniel first moved us over to Sun Life from the old Manulife plan — I think that was sometime in 2018, maybe late summer? I had just finished my third season at GreenLeaf and we were starting to pick up more commercial contracts.
- Then there was that headache in 2022 when Emily had the emergency C‑section with Lily at St. Joseph’s Healthcare Hamilton on May 6. At the admitting desk we found out my family coverage hadn’t been updated properly after Sophie’s birth, so Lily’s hospital and pediatric bills weren’t fully covered for the first three months until Sun Life fixed it in August 2022. I’m worried similar admin errors might happen again if the new plan isn’t set up correctly.

The form is asking how many years I’ve been continuously with the current carrier. Do they want me to count from when GreenLeaf first signed with Sun Life (which I *think* is around six or seven years now), or from when I added family coverage after Sophie was born?

I’m currently on modified duties and not sure how many hours I’ll realistically be able to work this year, so I really don’t want any holdups with claims.

Thanks,
Michael

metadata.subject: Clarification on years with Sun Life for new enrollment form

artifacts.content:
From: Sarah Thompson <s.thompson@greenleafurban.ca>
To: Michael Chen <m.chen27@gmail.com>
Date: 2025-02-04 15:18
Subject: RE: Benefits enrollment – years with carrier question

Hi Michael,

Good questions.

For the “number of years with carrier” line on the insurer’s questionnaire, they’re really just looking for a rough sense of how long you’ve been under the same group policy, not an exact anniversary date.

According to our records, GreenLeaf’s group benefits first switched to Sun Life effective October 1, 2017. You joined the company in 2016, so you would have been moved from the old Manulife plan onto Sun Life automatically at that time. That means you’ve been under the Sun Life umbrella for just over seven years as of this past fall.

They don’t differentiate between when you were single vs. family coverage; it’s all considered continuous with the same carrier unless there was a full lapse. Even with the issue you mentioned around Lily’s birth at St. Joseph’s Healthcare Hamilton (where your family coverage didn’t get updated properly at first), Sun Life still treated you as an ongoing member of the plan.

So on the form, you can safely put “7” or “7+” years with the current carrier. They’re not going to reject anything if you’re off by a few months.

Let me know if you need help with any of the other sections about the nature of disability or prognosis before you send everything back to the adjuster.

Thanks,
Sarah
Office Manager
GreenLeaf Urban Landscaping Inc.

metadata.subject: RE: Benefits enrollment – years with carrier question

Location: Home – kitchen table
Attendees: Michael Chen; Emily Zhang

Description:

Call with claims rep from Sun Life to go through the long questionnaire they mailed after Daniel filed the WSIB incident report for my back. Need to be ready to talk through:

- How long I’ve been insured with the current carrier (Sarah said GreenLeaf switched to Sun Life back in fall 2017, so that’s around seven and a half years now under the same group plan, even though the family coverage pieces changed when Sophie and Lily were born).
- Nature of business – explain that our nature of business is commercial/residential landscaping and winter snow services, but because Emily works 12‑hour night shifts at St. Joseph’s Healthcare Hamilton and we can’t afford full‑time childcare, Daniel and I agreed in March 2024 that I’d skip most overnight snow call‑outs and focus on daytime maintenance and small installs.
- Nature of disability – my occupational lower back strain from lifting a full pallet of patio stones on Feb 20, 2025, and how WSIB opened a claim the next week.

metadata.title: Phone appointment – insurer questionnaire (years with carrier, disability details)

artifacts.content:
From: Sun Life Claims <no-reply@sunlife.ca>
To: Michael Chen <m.chen27@gmail.com>
Date: 2025-02-10 08:02
Subject: Follow-up: Disability claim questionnaire – policy history section

Dear Michael Chen,

Thank you for speaking with our representative on February 6, 2025, regarding your disability claim related to your occupational lower back strain. This email is to confirm the information we have on file for the policy history section of your questionnaire.

Our records indicate that you have been covered under your employer’s group benefits policy with Sun Life Financial since October 1, 2017, without interruption. During this time, your coverage level has changed (for example, from single to family coverage following the birth of your first child), but the carrier itself has remained the same. For the purposes of the “number of years with carrier” question, you may indicate that you have been insured with the current carrier for approximately seven years.

Please note that the administrative issue that occurred in May 2022, when your family coverage was not updated correctly at the time of your daughter Lily’s emergency C‑section at St. Joseph’s Healthcare Hamilton, did not constitute a lapse in coverage. Once the error was corrected in August 2022, all eligible expenses were processed according to the terms of the policy.

If you believe there has been any break in coverage (for example, a period longer than 31 days where premiums were not remitted), please contact your employer’s plan administrator so they can provide us with updated information.

Sincerely,
Sun Life Disability Claims Team

metadata.subject: Follow-up: Disability claim questionnaire – policy history section

Block off time while girls nap to finish the disability and health questionnaire from the insurer.

artifacts.content:
From: Jenna Morris <jenna.morris@maplebenefits.ca>
To: Michael Chen <m.chen@greenleafurban.ca>
Date: 2025-02-14 10:06
Subject: RE: Follow‑up – years with previous carrier

Hi Michael,

Nice talking with you and Emily yesterday. Following up on your question about what to enter for the “number of years with carrier” field on the small‑group quote.

Based on the documents you sent, GreenLeaf’s last fully active stretch with Sun Life ran for **a little over one year** after the mid‑2022 renewal before Daniel terminated the contract. The system doesn’t need decimals – you can safely put “1 year” in that field. The short‑term August 2023 arrangement Daniel tried to set up with Co‑operators doesn’t count as time “with carrier” because it was never actually issued; as you noted, they declined the plan after reviewing your July 2023 emergency room visit and the prior WSIB claims.

For the narrative questions, you can explain that you’re now mostly on lighter garden bed maintenance and tool repairs at the yard at 1420 Industrial Park Road due to the lower‑back disc issue from the July 2023 overexertion incident at 18 Kingfisher Drive in Stoney Creek. That context helps underwriters understand any modified duties.

Also, don’t forget to mention the brief lapse last summer when you and Emily had to juggle paying for prescriptions and physio out of pocket; that’s relevant to why you’re shopping around now.

Let me know once Sarah submits the online form and I’ll track it on my end.

Best,
Jenna
Senior Benefits Advisor, Maple Benefits Group

Subject: Forms, doctors, and random life admin

Hey Jas,

You asked how the paperwork grind is going, so here’s the fun update.

I’m stuck on this one WSIB form where they want the exact “date of disability” for when I stopped regular work after the patio stone incident. I keep thinking back to how we handled your stuff when you were doing that outpatient CBT program at CAMH. My younger brother, Jason Chen, and I do not share a household; you rent a condo in downtown Toronto, but from June to August 2024 you crashed on our couch at 27 Fairview Avenue three or four nights a week while you were doing the program after your panic attack at work, so for that summer you were semi‑living with us even though you kept your Toronto lease. Your forms also wanted super‑specific dates and we still kind of guessed.

For me, the injury itself was on that Saturday at the Burlington job, but I didn’t actually stay home until the following Monday. I’m leaning toward putting **January 22, 2025** as the official start, since that’s when I first had to leave a shift early and couldn’t finish the route.

Honestly, I haven’t pushed to go back full time yet because the modified schedule has actually been easier to manage with the kids and everything at home.

Anyway, hope work is calmer for you now.

Mike

Hi Sarah,

I’m working through the WSIB forms this morning and got stuck on the section asking for the “date of disability / when employee stopped working regular duties.” I want to make sure what I put lines up with what you have on file.

As you remember, my back really flared up during that patio stone job at the small plaza in Burlington near the end of January. I also see the form asks about any prior related issues. For background, GreenLeaf does year‑round grounds maintenance and small landscaping projects around Hamilton, but after the WSIB claim I filed in August 2023 for my back strain on the interlock job at 2210 Barton Street East, Daniel has been careful about assigning me to only lighter planting and mulching work and keeping me off the heavier skid steer and snow plow routes when my pain flares up in cold weather.

1) The official date you recorded as my first day off regular duties this time around; and
2) Whether we should reference the 2023 claim in this new paperwork or just note it as prior history?

Once I have those dates, I’ll finish the form and send you a scanned copy.

Michael

Title: WSIB – review "date of disability" section with Emily
Date: 2025-02-01 21:00–22:00
Location: Kitchen table – 27 Fairview Ave
Attendees: Michael Chen; Emily Zhang

Notes:

Go over the WSIB and insurance forms together after kids’ bedtime. Main goal is to double‑check all the dates around when my back issues got bad enough that I couldn’t keep doing regular landscaping work.

Things to clarify with Emily:
- When did I first start coming home really stiff from the Burlington plaza patio stone job? I remember complaining a lot the week of January 13, 2025.

metadata.title: WSIB – review "date of disability" section with Emily

artifacts.content:
From: Emily Zhang <e.zhang.rn@example.com>
To: Michael Chen <m.chen27@example.com>
Date: 2025-01-25 14:12
Subject: Doctor note & when you actually stopped working

Hey Mike,

I just finished reading the note from your family doctor at the Hamilton Family Health Team that you left on the counter. He wrote that you’ve been “off regular landscaping duties since approximately January 21, 2025 due to acute low back strain,” which should be fine for the insurance forms, even if it’s not the exact day you first felt it.

From my nurse brain, they mostly care that the date of disability lines up roughly with when you couldn’t safely do your usual GreenLeaf work anymore, not the first twinge you felt lifting those patio stones. You were still trying to push through for a couple of shifts after that, remember?

This whole thing reminds me of when my mom stayed with us from November 15 to December 5, 2023 at 27 Fairview to help after your minor relapse on oxycodone that your family doctor at the Hamilton Family Health Team flagged while you were off on modified leave; during that period she was essentially part of our household even though she normally lives in Markham. We had to dig up dates for that paperwork too and nobody cared if we were off by a day or two.

If you’re still unsure, email Sarah and ask what date payroll shows as your first day not on a full schedule. That’s probably the safest thing to list.

Love you,
Emily

Hi Michael,

Thanks for sending back the partially completed client questionnaire for the group benefits review. I’m going through the “client questions” section and noticed the line about the renewal date is still blank.

Our records from the broker show the current policy year running from **May 1, 2024 to April 30, 2025**, but Daniel mentioned there was some discussion last fall about shifting the renewal to line up better with our busy landscaping season. At one point, the broker floated **June 1, 2025** as a potential new renewal date if GreenLeaf wanted to change cycles, but I don’t see any confirmation that this was finalized.

Complicating things a bit is that from April 2024 to April 2025, WSIB has been reimbursing up to $1,200 per month for your rehab at the Hamilton Back & Pain Clinic under claim 24-HBP-1317, including the cognitive‑behavioural therapy sessions tied to your May 6, 2024 lifting injury. I just want to be sure any change to the renewal date doesn’t interfere with how that external coverage coordinates.

Can you check what you’ve been told by the broker and either write in **“policy renews May 1 each year”** or note the proposed **June 1, 2025** change so I can finish the form?

Thanks,
Sarah Thompson
Office Manager
GreenLeaf Urban Landscaping Inc.

P.S. Daniel also asked me to note that our nature of business includes a lot of seasonal snow clearing and salting in the winter, and when we’re shorthanded the crews sometimes rush to finish multiple sites in a short window, which the broker says can affect our risk profile.

metadata.subject: Clarification Needed – Group Benefits Renewal Questionnaire

artifacts.content:
Title: Call with Broker – Confirm Benefits Renewal Timing
Date: 2025-03-04 15:00–15:45
Location: Phone – Michael’s cell
Attendees: Michael Chen; Daniel McLeod; Jenna Park (Benefits Broker)

Description:

Agenda:
1. Confirm correct renewal date to enter in “client questions > renewal date” on insurer forms.
2. Discuss impact of modified/medical leave cases (including Michael’s) on eligibility.
3. Review coordination with WSIB and other third‑party payors.

Notes to self:
- Jenna’s last email said our current contract technically renews **on May 1 every year**, but she’s open to exploring a one‑time shift to **June 15, 2025** so that future renewals fall after the spring hiring rush.
- Need to ask whether my being on modified medical leave from GreenLeaf Urban Landscaping Inc. since March 18, 2024 due to the herniated disc at L4–L5 and chronic sciatica (Dr. Patel at Hamilton General Spine Clinic) affects the “active at work” wording. I’m still waiting on the MRI follow‑up scheduled for April 9, 2025 to see if surgery is on the table, so coverage has been a bit uncertain.
- Clarify whether the questionnaire wants the **contract anniversary date (May 1)** or the **billing adjustment date (June 15)** in that renewal field.

Reminders:
- Ask Daniel if he’s comfortable if I just write “Renews May 1 annually – potential shift to June 15, 2025 under discussion” on the draft form until we get final confirmation.
- Mention that I’m not back at full duties yet because my boss and I agreed it’s safer if I stay on modified work indefinitely until we see how things go.

metadata.title: Call with Broker – Confirm Benefits Renewal Timing

artifacts.content:
From: jenna.park@oakridgebenefits.ca
To: daniel@greenleafurban.ca; michael.chen@greenleafurban.ca
Date: 2025-03-06 10:02
Subject: Follow‑up – Renewal Timeline & Questionnaire Details

Hi Daniel and Michael,

Great speaking with you both on Tuesday.

To recap: your current group benefits policy is in force for the term **May 1, 2024 to April 30, 2025**. Unless we process a cycle change, the **official renewal date remains May 1**. The idea of moving the cycle to better match your landscaping season is still just a proposal. If you decide to go ahead, the earliest practical effective date I can arrange would be **June 15, 2025**, but that would only change future cycles, not the date you should list on the insurer’s “client questions > renewal date” line for this year’s review.

Michael, you mentioned that from April 2024 to April 2025 WSIB is reimbursing up to $1,200 per month for your rehab at the Hamilton Back & Pain Clinic (including CBT sessions for work‑related anxiety under claim 24‑HBP‑1317). That external funding doesn’t alter the group plan’s renewal, but we do need to ensure the coordination‑of‑benefits section of the questionnaire reflects that another party is paying some of your coverage.

For now, when you’re completing the form, please enter **“05/01/2025”** or simply “May 1, 2025” in the renewal date field. If we later confirm a mid‑June cycle change, I’ll send updated wording for next year’s paperwork.

Best,
Jenna Park
Senior Benefits Consultant
Oakridge Benefits Consulting

Title: Deadline – Submit Completed Client Questionnaire to Broker
Date: 2025-03-20 11:30–12:00
Location: Home office desk
Attendees: Michael Chen

Description:

Task list before this deadline:
- Double‑check all sections of the insurer’s client questionnaire, especially:
  • “Information About Your Client” – include note that Emily started picking up an extra 0.5 FTE of night shifts on the surgical floor at St. Joseph’s Healthcare Hamilton back in October 2023 so we could keep up with the $1,350 monthly rent at 27 Fairview Avenue and the $280/month Niagara College loan payments after my hours at GreenLeaf were reduced.
  • “Nature Of Disability” – explain that the lower back soft tissue injury with chronic pain flares really ramped up after the late March 2025 lifting incident, which forced Emily to switch to more night shifts so she could help me with basic tasks like getting out of bed and putting on my work boots.
  • “Prognosis” – copy wording from Dr. Laura Bennett’s Feb 3, 2025 report about good prospects of returning to full‑time work if I stick with biweekly CBT for another 3 months to manage anxiety and sleep issues from the October 21, 2024 workplace injury.
- For the “client questions > renewal date” line, Jenna said to use **May 1, 2025** as the renewal date this cycle, even though Daniel is still thinking about a possible shift to **June 15** for future years.

Reminder: Email the scanned form to Jenna before noon and cc Daniel so he knows the renewal paperwork is in.

metadata.title: Deadline – Submit Completed Client Questionnaire to Broker

artifacts.content:
From: anjali.patel@hhsc.ca
To: michael.chen@greenleafurban.ca
Date: 2025-02-28 08:47
Subject: Insurance Forms – Missing Details

Dear Mr. Chen,

I’ve reviewed the disability and benefits forms you left with my office. Most sections are complete, but the insurer’s “client questions” page has a blank where it asks for the **renewal date of your employer’s group coverage**.

In your notes, you wrote that the policy year currently runs from **May 1, 2024 to April 30, 2025**, and that your employer’s broker is considering changing the cycle so that future renewals fall in mid‑June (you mentioned **June 15, 2025** as a tentative date). However, the medical section I must sign should reflect the renewal date that is actually in force, not a proposed change.

Please confirm with your employer or broker whether you should list **“May 1, 2025”** or another firm date. Once you fill that in, my office can finalize the medical portion. As you explained, you’ve been on modified medical leave from GreenLeaf Urban Landscaping Inc. since March 18, 2024 because of the herniated disc at L4–L5 and chronic sciatica in your left leg, and your MRI follow‑up is booked for April 9, 2025 to determine if surgery is required. Having the correct renewal date is important so the insurer can assess ongoing eligibility during this period.

Kindly return the completed page to my clinic, and we will submit everything together.

Sincerely,
Dr. Anjali Patel
Hamilton General Hospital Spine Clinic

From: benefits@sunlife.ca
To: Michael Chen <m.chen27@gmail.com>
Date: 2025-05-28 09:14
Subject: Follow-up on LTD coverage and upcoming renewal

Dear Michael Jiahao Chen,

Thank you for speaking with me on the phone yesterday about your Long-Term Disability (LTD) and extended health benefits through GreenLeaf Urban Landscaping Inc.

As discussed, your current group benefits policy is scheduled for its annual review at the end of June 2025. For clarity, this refers to the employer’s group contract anniversary, not the specific renewal date on your individual claim file, which will be confirmed separately in writing once underwriting completes its review. Please do not assume the claim renewal will automatically align with the group anniversary, as those dates can differ.

I understand your concerns given your previous experience in Spring 2021, when your short‑term disability claim related to anxiety and depression treatment with counsellor Mark Ellis at the Hamilton Community Mental Health Centre was initially denied under a pre‑existing condition clause. We are taking that history into account while we reassess your current file.

You also mentioned that GreenLeaf Urban Landscaping Inc. is currently your only source of income while you are on modified medical leave following the lumbar disc herniation diagnosed at the Hamilton General Hospital spine clinic in March 2024, and that your employer, Daniel McLeod, has you on a 3‑day‑per‑week light‑duty schedule in the Nash Road yard. This information has been added to your file.

Once the claim‑specific renewal timeline is finalized, we will send you a separate letter outlining the exact date and any conditions. Until then, please refer only to the “end of June 2025 group review” as a general marker, not as your formal claim renewal date.

Sincerely,

Rachel Porter
Senior Case Manager, Sun Life

metadata.subject: Follow-up on LTD coverage and upcoming renewal

artifacts.content:
Title: Call with HR re: benefits renewal questions
Date: 2025-06-10 14:00–14:45
Location: Phone – Michael’s cell
Attendees: Michael Chen; Sarah Thompson (GreenLeaf Office Manager)

Agenda / Notes:
- Clarify timing of GreenLeaf Urban Landscaping Inc. benefits cycle vs. Michael’s individual claim review.
- Sarah confirmed that the company’s group benefits contract with the insurer is set for its next policy-year rollover on June 30, 2025. She emphasized that this is an internal plan anniversary, not necessarily the official renewal date on Michael’s disability claim paperwork.
- Michael to wait for insurer’s written notice specifying his own claim renewal timeline; Sarah said it “might be a few weeks before or after the plan rollover,” depending on processing.
- Discuss impact of ongoing modified duties: Michael is still on 3-day-per-week light work in the Nash Road yard while he continues physiotherapy for his lumbar disc herniation that was diagnosed at the Hamilton General Hospital spine clinic in March 2024.
- Michael mentioned he’s not comfortable taking on heavier jobs again because he’s worried he might flare things up and have to go off work all over again, so he wants to understand whether any change in status around the plan’s rollover could affect income replacement.
- Sarah suggested Michael keep copies of all medical notes and the original WSIB decision, as the insurer may ask for updated documentation around the time they reassess his claim.

Reminder: This meeting is for general benefits timing; exact claim renewal date to come directly from insurer in a separate letter.

metadata.title: Call with HR re: benefits renewal questions

artifacts.content:
From: Daniel McLeod <daniel.mcleod@greenleafurban.ca>
To: Michael Chen <m.chen27@gmail.com>
Date: 2025-05-06 16:22
Subject: Benefits review meeting before summer season

Hey Michael,

Hope the back is holding up and physiotherapy on Upper James is still helping. Before we get into the busy summer rush, I’d like to sit down with you and Sarah to go over where things stand with your modified duties and benefits.

Our broker reminded me that the GreenLeaf group plan does its annual benefits review in late June each year. Last year the review paperwork landed around June 25, so I’m expecting something similar this year, but the broker said the insurer sets your individual claim renewal date separately and will send that directly to you. In other words, don’t assume that your claim renews on the same day the company plan rolls over.

I know you’re still on the 3‑day‑a‑week light‑duty schedule in the Nash Road yard after the lumbar disc herniation you aggravated lifting those 80 lb patio stones on the Stoney Creek job. Given everything, and the fact that GreenLeaf Urban Landscaping Inc. is currently your only source of income, I want to make sure there are no surprises when the insurer does their claim review.

We can also talk about the old informal written warning from November 2022 after that overtime argument in the yard. It doesn’t affect your benefits, but I’d rather clear the air so you’re not worrying about it when the insurer asks about your employment history.

Can you do next Wednesday at 3:30 p.m. at the office?

Thanks,
Daniel

metadata.subject: Benefits review meeting before summer season

artifacts.content:
From: Emily Zhang <emily.zhangRN@gmail.com>
To: Michael Chen <m.chen27@gmail.com>
Date: 2025-04-30 21:03
Subject: Notes for your insurance questionnaire

Mike,

I typed up some notes you might want when you fill out that big insurance form your case manager sent. You said there was a question about “renewal date,” but the letter they mailed only said your disability claim will be reviewed “mid‑summer 2025,” so maybe just write that and leave the exact date blank until they confirm.

Don’t forget to mention how everything with Sophie has affected how you look at the benefits. When our 5‑year‑old, Sophie Chen, was evaluated for a possible developmental delay at the McMaster Children’s Hospital developmental pediatrics clinic in January 2024, after her junior kindergarten teacher at King’s Forest Public School raised concerns about speech and fine motor skills, we were both shocked by how fast therapy costs add up.

Also, you should explain why you’re nervous about the insurer being picky. During your anxiety and depression leave in Spring 2021, when you were seeing counsellor Mark Ellis at the Hamilton Community Mental Health Centre every Thursday at 5:30 p.m., Sun Life initially denied your short‑term disability claim over that pre‑existing condition clause. No wonder you’re double‑checking everything now.

Finally, make sure they understand that since the lower back soft tissue injury with spasms from the March 2025 lifting incident at the GreenLeaf job site, your family doctor put you on naproxen and that drowsy muscle relaxant, and you even had to ask our neighbour Mark to handle Sophie’s school drop‑offs.

Love,
Em

Title: Phone appointment – Insurer case manager (claim review timing)
Date: 2025-06-03 10:30–11:00
Location: Kitchen table – quiet for call
Attendees: Michael Chen; Sun Life case manager (Rachel)

Description / Prep notes:
- Purpose: Rachel to explain how the timing works for the upcoming review of my disability claim and when I can expect them to set a formal renewal date.
- She said in her last email that the GreenLeaf Urban Landscaping Inc. group plan is reviewed “end of June,” but that my specific claim review might be scheduled a few weeks before or after that. She told me not to write any specific date on forms until I get their official letter.
- I want to ask whether my parents staying over affects anything. My mom, Lan, has been spending 3–4 nights a week at our place on Fairview Avenue since my dad Wei’s mild stroke in March 2023 at Trillium Health Partners – Mississauga Hospital, mostly to help with childcare when my pain flares and I have physiotherapy.
- Also need to mention that since the lower back soft tissue injury in March 2025 at the GreenLeaf job site, my family doctor at Fairview Medical Clinic put me on naproxen and a mild muscle relaxant that makes me drowsy, so our neighbour Mark has been doing Sophie’s morning school drop‑offs for the past month.
- Clarify whether any of this household/support stuff changes how they look at my claim when they decide the next review cycle.

Reminder to self: Ask Rachel to email a summary after the call instead of giving an exact date over the phone so I have it in writing.
